summary: Everything we have done so far was just setting the stage for our real goal, which is getting our hands dirty with the Rust programming language. It’s a modern engineering marvel destined to make all other languages obsolete. Either we jump on the Rust wagon now or get left behind with the memory-unsafe losers.
title: Diving into Rust

content:
We now have a working system with several tools installed but in most cases we want these tools to work together and reach a specific state before we can start using them. Once we define what that state should look like we also need a simple way to create and tear down environments as needed.
For my use case, that state includes a Kubernetes cluster running Traefik, Prometheus and Grafana. I automate the environment creation process with a Bash script and Terraform. To run the script you will need a domain with DNS records pointing to your homelab’s IP and a wildcard certificate.
To download the script run the following..
USERNAME="user" curl -Lo /home/${USERNAME}/.scripts/tfreset.sh https://raw.githubusercontent.com/korliaftis/korliaftis.github.io/master/scripts/minikube/tfreset.sh chmod +x /home/${USERNAME}/.scripts/tfreset.sh Before running the script, make sure to edit it and update the following variables..
DOMAIN_NAME="" # your domain name - "domain.xyz" CERTIFICATE_FILE_PATH="" # the full path to the certificate - "/home/${USERNAME}/.ssl/fullchain.cer" PRIVATE_KEY_FILE_PATH="" # the full path to the private key - "/home/${USERNAME}/.ssl/${DOMAIN_NAME}.key" In summary, the script first performs a few preflight checks to ensure everything is in order, it then generates Terraform manifests in the ~/.project/minikube directory and finally applies them in two stages to avoid dependency errors.
When you run the script it will delete all existing Minikube clusters and contents from the ~/.project/minikube directory.
In the following sections we will see more details about some key components.
minikube We use Minikube with the Docker driver. During the installation we created it’s network so the nodes will receive IPs from that subnet.
We can change the number of nodes that will be created by editing the variables section of the script and updating the following value..
MINIKUBE_NODES="3" Some of the workloads we deploy require at least 2 nodes otherwise some pods will not be scheduled and remain in a Pending state.
haproxy To access HTTP workloads running on Minikube we use the HAProxy we have installed on our host system.
It listens on ports 80 and 443 of the host system and forwards all incoming traffic to the “minikube” Docker network on ports 31080 and 31443 respectively. For this setup to work, the Kubernetes Ingress Controller (Istio, Traefik, etc) must be configured to create NodePort services on those ports.
We have configured HAProxy with active TCP healthchecks so it routes traffic only to backends that have a server running. I have tried some alternatives to HAProxy but none of them could do this simple thing out of the box.. for example NGINX only offers passive TCP healthchecks in it’s free version and for active TCP healthchecks we need to use their paid version. I also tried Traefik but as of today it doesn’t support any TCP healthchecks for it’s backends.
In general HAProxy just works, we can access it’s dashboard in port 8404 and view it’s logs by doing..
sudo journalctl -u haproxy -f One alternative approach to the whole “reverse proxy in front of Minikube” thing would be to use MetalLB but I could not make it work consistently with Minikube. That said, it’s been some time since I last tested it so it may work great now.
One disadvantage of this setup is that port 80 and 443 of the host system are “permanently” bound to HAProxy even when Minikube is not running. If this is something that bothers you, disable the autostart of the HAProxy systemd service and start it only when you need it or even better use a different network interface for HAproxy.
traefik As discussed in the HAProxy section, our Ingress Controller needs to use NodePort services on ports 31080 and 3144 so we configure this using the Traefik Helm chart values file.
Another thing we configure is a K8s secret on the traefik-system namespace with the certificate for our domain. Traefik will use this secret to create a TLSStore which will handle TLS for our domain automatically.
After the installation an IngressClass with the name “traefik” will be created and it will be configured to be the default IngressClass of the cluster. This means that we don’t have to set the ingressClassName: field in every Ingress resource we create.
If in the future we decide to use multiple Ingress Controllers we can use this field to select which one will handle each Ingress in a case by case basis..
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: Ingress metadata: # [...] spec: ingressClassName: "traefik" # [...] One final note, Traefik evaluates routing rules based on their length and not based on their “specificity” meaning that if we create an Ingress for the host blue.domain.xyz and another one for the host *.domain.xyz the rules that will be created will be..
Host('blue.domain.xyz' HostRegexp('{subdomain:[a-zA-Z0-9-]+}.domain.xyz') The second rule is longer so it will be evaluated first and since it will match the request to blue.domain.xyz it will always take precedence over the more specific route.
To solve this we can manually set the priority on the Ingress using an annotation..
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: Ingress metadata: # [...] annotations: traefik.ingress.kubernetes.io/router.priority: "1" # [...] spec: # [...] The priority can be any value between 1 and 1000 (higher is better). We could either annotate the “wildcard” Ingress with a low priority or annotate the “non-wildcard” Ingress with a high priority, either way our problem will be solved.
prometheus We install 2 Helm charts related to Prometheus, the prometheus-operator-crds and the kube-prometheus-stack.
We mostly use default values for both except from the way the Prometheus operator detects ServiceMonitor resources. The default behavior is for Prometheus to scrape a ServiceMonitor only when it has matching labels. This is useful if we have multiple Prometheus operators on the same cluster.
In summary the way it works is the following..
if serviceMonitorSelectorNilUsesHelmValues: false is configured in the values file we need to run kubectl get prometheuses.monitoring.coreos.com -n prometheus-system -o yaml | grep -A8 serviceMonitorSelector and add the output as labels to our ServiceMonitor if serviceMonitorSelectorNilUsesHelmValues: true is configured in the values file the ServiceMonitor will be scraped automatically In either case the service monitors can be in any namespace.
After the installation we can access Prometheus in the URL prometheus.${DOMAIN_NAME} and Alertmanager in the URL alertmanager.${DOMAIN_NAME}.
grafana For the Grafana Helm chart we set the username to “admin” and password to ""
We can access Grafana in the URL grafana.${DOMAIN_NAME} and login with the following credentials..
username: admin password: asdf12#$ To provision dashboards we use a configmap that Terraform creates with the contents of the “dashboards” directory that the script creates.
Note that since we don’t use “persistence”, any dashboard that is not provisioned by the configmap or any configuration that is not coming from the Helm chart values file will be lost if the Grafana pod restarts.
troubleshooting If it doesn’t work, start troubleshooting the path of the requests..
Is DNS pointing to the IP of our system? dig ${DOMAIN_NAME} dig *.${DOMAIN_NAME} Does HAProxy see the backends as online? go with your browser to the IP of your system in port 8404 - http://192.168.1.2:8404 Do the HAProxy logs show our connection attempts? sudo journalctl -u haproxy -f Have all the Helm charts installed correctly? helm list -Aa Are all the pods running? kubectl get pods -A This is not an exhaustive list of everything that could have gone wrong but most likely it’s one of these things.
ongoing maintenance To keep everything happy we need to keep up to date the..
Terraform providers
kubernetes helm Helm charts
traefik traefik-crds kube-prometheus-stack prometheus-operator-crds grafana Finally, if you have not automated the process, don’t forget to generate a new TLS certificate for your domain every 3 months.
summary:
We now have a working system with several tools installed but in most cases we want these tools to work together and reach a specific state before we can start using them. Once we define what that state should look like we also need a simple way to create and tear down environments as needed.

For my use case, that state includes a Kubernetes cluster running Traefik, Prometheus and Grafana. I automate the environment creation process with a Bash script and Terraform. To run the script you will need a domain with DNS records pointing to your homelab’s IP and a wildcard certificate.
title: Connecting the Dots

content:
Since I started working with computers I always loved the idea of a homelab, a safe space to mess with stuff, break stuff and sometimes learn a few things along the way.
Back when I was trying to land my first tech job my homelab was a bunch of noisy Cisco switches and routers. Along the way I discovered GNS3 and eventually those physical switches and routers were virtualized on my beloved Sony Vaio VGN-N31Z with it’s Core 2 Duo, 2GB of RAM and 120GB of spinning rust.
A few years later when I started working with Windows my homelab transitioned into a Windows Server 2012 R2 VM running on VirtualBox. It didn’t take long to realize that if I wanted to run more than just a couple of VMs at the same time I would need some serious hardware. So I ended up breaking the bank on a desktop running the free version of VMware ESXi packed with an Intel i7, 64GB of RAM and a bunch of SSDs.
Fast forward again and with everything moving to the public cloud my homelab shifted to an AWS account. What was once a capital expense with finite resources became an operational expense with “limitless” capabilities. However, this newfound flexibility quickly turned into a source of stress thanks to its ever-increasing fees and charges.
These days my homelab is a “vintage” Dell Latitude with an Intel i3, 16GB of RAM and a 1TB SSD running Debian 12.
Foundation This guide begins after you have selected the hardware for your homelab, downloaded a Debian 12 ISO, created a bootable USB and completed a minimal installation with only the “standard system utilities” and “SSH server” selected. By now you should know your machine’s IP address on the network and have successfully logged in via SSH.
Let’s get started..
switch to the root user..
su - set the hostname..
HOSTNAME="server" hostnamectl set-hostname ${HOSTNAME} set the timezone..
TIMEZONE_REGION="Europe" TIMEZONE_CITY="Athens" timedatectl set-timezone ${TIMEZONE_REGION}/${TIMEZONE_CITY} disable the cdrom source (if we used the DVD/USB Debian image)..
sed -i '/^#/! {/cdrom/ s/^/# /}' /etc/apt/sources.list set the default locale..
apt install -y locales-all echo 'LC_ALL="en_US.UTF-8"' >> /etc/default/locale install packages..
apt update && apt upgrade -y apt install -y bash-completion ca-certificates apt-transport-https vim git curl jq gnupg unzip htop tree tmux nmap tcpdump ethtool strace configure sudo..
USERNAME="user" apt install -y sudo usermod -a -G sudo ${USERNAME} enable sudo without password..
USERNAME="user" echo "${USERNAME} ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD:ALL" | EDITOR='tee -a' visudo visudo --check disable the laptop lid function (if we use a laptop)..
echo 'HandleLidSwitch=ignore' >> /etc/systemd/logind.conf suppress the output of kernel logs on the console..
echo 'kernel.printk = 1 4 1 7' >> /etc/sysctl.conf sysctl -p increase the /proc/sys/fs/inotify/max_user_instances value..
echo 'fs.inotify.max_user_instances = 16384' >> /etc/sysctl.conf sysctl -p change the way the bash prompt looks..
USERNAME="user" echo 'export PS1="\[\e[37m\][\[\e[93m\]\w\[\e[37m\]] \[\e[0m\]"' >> /home/${USERNAME}/.bashrc echo 'export PS1="\[\e[37m\][\[\e[91m\]\w\[\e[37m\]] \[\e[0m\]"' >> /root/.bashrc create a few files and directories to keep our stuff organized..
USERNAME="user" su - ${USERNAME} -c "touch /home/${USERNAME}/.bash_aliases" su - ${USERNAME} -c "mkdir /home/${USERNAME}/.project" su - ${USERNAME} -c "mkdir /home/${USERNAME}/.ssl" su - ${USERNAME} -c "mkdir /home/${USERNAME}/.scripts" echo 'export PATH="$PATH:/home/user/.scripts"' >> /home/${USERNAME}/.bashrc create an ssh key for our user account..
USERNAME="user" su - ${USERNAME} -c "ssh-keygen -t rsa -b 4096" su - ${USERNAME} -c "touch /home/${USERNAME}/.ssh/config" chmod 600 /home/${USERNAME}/.ssh/config update the password of our user account..
USERNAME="user" passwd ${USERNAME} update the password of the root account..
passwd root Building Blocks Now that we’ve established the foundation, we can begin adding our building blocks.
Since I work as a Site Reliability Engineer the tools I rely on include the following..
docker USERNAME="user" curl -L https://download.docker.com/linux/debian/gpg | gpg --dearmor > /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.gpg echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/docker.gpg] https://download.docker.com/linux/debian $(. /etc/os-release && echo "$VERSION_CODENAME") stable" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/docker.list apt update && apt install -y docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io docker-buildx-plugin docker-compose-plugin usermod -aG docker ${USERNAME} systemctl daemon-reload systemctl enable docker.service systemctl start docker.service By default Docker binds published ports to all interfaces (0.0.0.0) making containers accessible from anywhere. I prefer the opposite, containers should be private unless explicitly exposed.
systemctl stop docker.service cat << 'EOF' > /etc/docker/daemon.json { "ip": "127.0.0.1", "metrics-addr": "0.0.0.0:9323" } EOF systemctl start docker.service kubectl TEMP_DIR="$(mktemp -d)" curl -sLo ${TEMP_DIR}/kubectl "https://dl.k8s.io/release/$(curl -L -s https://dl.k8s.io/release/stable.txt)/bin/linux/amd64/kubectl" install -o root -g root -m 0755 ${TEMP_DIR}/kubectl /usr/local/bin/kubectl kubectl completion bash > /etc/bash_completion.d/kubectl k9s TEMP_DIR="$(mktemp -d)" SYSTEM_ARCH="$(dpkg --print-architecture)" K9S_VERSION="$(curl -s https://api.github.com/repos/derailed/k9s/releases/latest | jq -r .tag_name)" curl -sLo ${TEMP_DIR}/k9s_Linux_${SYSTEM_ARCH}.tar.gz https://github.com/derailed/k9s/releases/download/${K9S_VERSION}/k9s_Linux_${SYSTEM_ARCH}.tar.gz tar -xzf ${TEMP_DIR}/k9s_Linux_${SYSTEM_ARCH}.tar.gz --directory ${TEMP_DIR} cp ${TEMP_DIR}/k9s /usr/local/bin/k9s k9s completion bash > /etc/bash_completion.d/k9s minikube TEMP_DIR="$(mktemp -d)" curl -sLo ${TEMP_DIR}/minikube_latest_amd64.deb https://storage.googleapis.com/minikube/releases/latest/minikube_latest_amd64.deb dpkg -i ${TEMP_DIR}/minikube_latest_amd64.deb minikube completion bash > /etc/bash_completion.d/minikube When Minikube starts it checks for a Docker network with the same name and creates it automatically if it doesn’t exist. By creating this network manually we can define a custom IP range making the node IPs predictable.
docker network create "minikube" --driver="bridge" --subnet="10.20.30.0/24" --gateway="10.20.30.1" haproxy A simple reverse proxy to easily access HTTP workloads running on Minikube. It listens on ports 80 and 443 of the system and forwards connections to the Minikube network.
apt install -y haproxy systemctl daemon-reload systemctl enable haproxy.service systemctl stop haproxy.service cp /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg.bak cat << 'EOF' > /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg global log stdout format raw local0 info defaults log global timeout client 30000 timeout connect 5000 timeout server 30000 frontend stats mode http bind *:8404 stats enable stats uri / stats refresh 30s no log frontend metrics mode http bind *:8405 http-request use-service prometheus-exporter no log frontend haproxy-http mode tcp bind *:80 default_backend minikube-http option tcplog frontend haproxy-https mode tcp bind *:443 default_backend minikube-https option tcplog backend minikube-http mode tcp server minikube 10.20.30.2:31080 check server minikube-m02 10.20.30.3:31080 check server minikube-m03 10.20.30.4:31080 check server minikube-m04 10.20.30.5:31080 check server minikube-m05 10.20.30.6:31080 check backend minikube-https mode tcp server minikube 10.20.30.2:31443 check server minikube-m02 10.20.30.3:31443 check server minikube-m03 10.20.30.4:31443 check server minikube-m04 10.20.30.5:31443 check server minikube-m05 10.20.30.6:31443 check EOF haproxy -c -f /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg systemctl start haproxy.service helm curl -L https://baltocdn.com/helm/signing.asc | gpg --dearmor > /etc/apt/keyrings/helm.gpg echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/helm.gpg] https://baltocdn.com/helm/stable/debian/ all main" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/helm.list apt update && apt install -y helm helm completion bash > /etc/bash_completion.d/helm terraform USERNAME="user" curl -L https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com/gpg | gpg --dearmor > /etc/apt/keyrings/hashicorp.gpg echo "deb [signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/hashicorp.gpg] https://apt.releases.hashicorp.com $(lsb_release -cs) main" > /etc/apt/sources.list.d/hashicorp.list apt update && apt install -y terraform echo 'complete -C /usr/bin/terraform terraform' >> /home/${USERNAME}/.bashrc act TEMP_DIR="$(mktemp -d)" SYSTEM_ARCH="$(uname -m)" ACT_VERSION="$(curl -s https://api.github.com/repos/nektos/act/releases/latest | jq -r .tag_name)" curl -sLo ${TEMP_DIR}/act_Linux_${SYSTEM_ARCH}.tar.gz https://github.com/nektos/act/releases/download/${ACT_VERSION}/act_Linux_${SYSTEM_ARCH}.tar.gz tar -xzf ${TEMP_DIR}/act_Linux_${SYSTEM_ARCH}.tar.gz --directory ${TEMP_DIR} cp ${TEMP_DIR}/act /usr/local/bin/act Ongoing Maintenance Setting things up once is fine but without updates we are stuck with a “snapshot” of the system from the day it was installed.
Running apt update && apt upgrade -y regularly will keep both the system and most of the tools up to date.
To update kubectl, k9s, minikube and act we can simply run their installation commands again.
Tactical Enhancements This part of the guide covers things that are nice to have but not strictly necessary.
A nice upgrade for our homelab is registering a domain, it doesn’t matter if it’s a .com or a .xyz. With a domain we can set up DNS records that work consistently across devices, like A records (domain.xyz and *.domain.xyz) pointing to our homelab’s internal IP. We can also use Cloudflare’s free tier to manage DNS and take advantage of it’s API and with an ACME client issue wildcard TLS certificates for our domain.
I use acme.sh for this. By providing my Cloudflare Zone ID, Account ID and API Token along with the domain name a valid certificate is generated and saved to my ~/.ssl directory.
DOMAIN_NAME="" CF_ZONE_ID="" CF_ACCOUNT_ID="" CF_TOKEN="" USERNAME="user" TEMP_DIR="$(mktemp -d)" docker run \ --rm \ --interactive \ --tty \ --env CF_Zone_ID="${CF_ZONE_ID}" \ --env CF_Account_ID="${CF_ACCOUNT_ID}" \ --env CF_Token="${CF_TOKEN}" \ --volume ${TEMP_DIR}/ssl:/acme.sh \ neilpang/acme.sh:latest \ acme.sh \ --dns dns_cf \ --domain ${DOMAIN_NAME} \ --domain *.${DOMAIN_NAME} \ --issue \ --keylength 4096 \ --server letsencrypt cp -r ${TEMP_DIR}/ssl/${DOMAIN_NAME}/* /home/${USERNAME}/.ssl chown -R ${USERNAME}:${USERNAME} /home/${USERNAME}/.ssl/* rm -rf ${TEMP_DIR} history -c && history -w Another useful upgrade for our homelab is setting up remote access. We could rent a VPS and set up WireGuard tunnels between our devices but this adds latency. A better option is using something like Nebula where the VPS can act as a broker and not as a relay. However, the simplest choice is to use a managed services like Tailscale or ZeroTier which will handle everything for us.
The last upgrade to consider is configuring wake-on-lan for our homelab to power it on remotely. Most systems support it, we just need to enable it in the BIOS. Pair wake-on-lan with an always-on Raspberry Pi on the same network and a remote access solution like the ones mentioned above and we will be able to connect to our homelab from anywhere, anytime.
Closing Thoughts Allright, we saw a bunch of things and touched many topics but one thing we did not cover is security.
So is it secure? Well, we prioritized convenience over security in a few areas but at the end of the day this is a homelab not a production system, it will never store sensitive data and if someone breaks into my house to steal my stuff my trusty old Dell Latitude will be the least of my concerns.
At the end of the day this is all about experimentation and learning so take what works for you and make it your own.
summary:
Since I started working with computers I always loved the idea of a homelab, a safe space to mess with stuff, break stuff and sometimes learn a few things along the way.

Back when I was trying to land my first tech job my homelab was a bunch of noisy Cisco switches and routers. Along the way I discovered GNS3 and eventually those physical switches and routers were virtualized on my beloved Sony Vaio VGN-N31Z with it’s Core 2 Duo, 2GB of RAM and 120GB of spinning rust.
title: Laying the Foundation
